coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots, carbon nano onions, and carbon nano horns, exhibit exceptional properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, and biocompatibility. These characteristics make them attractive for various biomedical applications, particularly in bioimaging and biosensing. In bioimaging, carbon-based nanomaterials offer several advantages. Their unique properties enable precise imaging of biological events in cells, tissues, and organs, with minimal interference in living processes. By leveraging these properties, researchers can achieve high-resolution imaging for accurate diagnosis and therapy. Similarly, carbon-based nanomaterials are crucial in biosensing applications. They provide a platform for developing highly sensitive biosensors due to their distinctive electrical properties and surface characteristics. Functionalizing these nanomaterials with specific biomolecules or receptors allows for the detection and extraction of target molecules with exceptional precision and sensitivity, inspired by principles found in living systems. A review article focusing on the applications of carbon-based nanomaterials in bioimaging and biosensing would provide insights into their potential in advancing biotechnology. This article discusses a variety of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots, carbon nano onions, and carbon nano horns, emphasizing their unique properties and applications in bioimaging and sensing. The review emphasizes the importance of detection accuracy and sensitivity in biomedical applications and investigates how carbon-based nanomaterials help to advance biotechnology. This exploration will allow researchers to explore and create novel diagnostic and therapeutic methods.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots, carbon nano onions, and carbon nano horns, exhibit exceptional properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, and biocompatibility. These characteristics make them attractive for various biomedical applications, particularly in bioimaging and biosensing. In bioimaging, carbon-based nanomaterials offer several advantages. Their unique properties enable precise imaging of biological events in cells, tissues, and organs, with minimal interference in living processes. By leveraging these properties, researchers can achieve high-resolution imaging for accurate diagnosis and therapy. Similarly, carbon-based nanomaterials are crucial in biosensing applications. They provide a platform for developing highly sensitive biosensors due to their distinctive electrical properties and surface characteristics. Functionalizing these nanomaterials with specific biomolecules or receptors allows for the detection and extraction of target molecules with exceptional precision and sensitivity, inspired by principles found in living systems. A review article focusing on the applications of carbon-based nanomaterials in bioimaging and biosensing would provide insights into their potential in advancing biotechnology. This article discusses a variety of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots, carbon nano onions, and carbon nano horns, emphasizing their unique properties and applications in bioimaging and sensing. The review emphasizes the importance of detection accuracy and sensitivity in biomedical applications and investigates how carbon-based nanomaterials help to advance biotechnology. This exploration will allow researchers to explore and create novel diagnostic and therapeutic methods.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is employed in a variety of science-related domains, including chemistry, physics, biology, and engineering. Its goal is to comprehend and manage materials at the nanoscale [1,2]. Numerous nanomaterials have been developed as a result of recent technical advancements. Nanomaterials range in size from 1 to 100 nm [3,4]. They may exist in a single dimension or several dimensions. A variety of techniques are used to detect the particle sizes of carbon nanomaterials, each with its own set of advantages in the analysis of these materials. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) allows high-resolution imaging and accurate examination at the nanometer scale. Meanwhile, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) offers clarified surface imaging and size estimation, which is especially useful for larger sample areas. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) determines hydrodynamic size distribution in solution, providing insight into particle behavior in liquid environments. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) provides high-resolution imaging of the individual particles on surfaces, revealing important details about their morphology. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS) provide structural information by indirectly indicating particle size and shape. Nanoparticle Tracking Analysis (NTA) uses Brownian motion to study size distribution in solution [5]. Graphene and its derivatives, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerene, carbon dots (CDs), carbon nano onions (CNOs), and carbon nano horns (CNHs) are examples of carbon-based nanomaterials that have recently received attention for their potential use in the biomedical field due to their distinctive physical, chemical, optical, and mechanical properties [6].

In the fields of medicines, diagnostics, imaging, and regenerative medicine, nanomaterials provide distinctive benefits and prospects [7]. Due to their unique electrical, optical, mechanical, and thermal characteristics, carbon-based nanomaterials such carbon nanotubes, graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanodots, carbon nano onions, and carbon nano horns have received a lot of notice recently in the field of nanomedicine [8,9]. As a result, multiple studies have endeavoured to use these materials in bioimaging and biosensors. CNTs are graphitic tubes that can have hemi fullerenes added to the ends as caps. SWCNTs are made up of a single graphene sheet, while MWCNTs are made up of many sheets that are layered and centred to one another. Both varieties of CNTs are nanoscale in size and exhibit an extremely high aspect ratio—that is, the ratio between the material's length and diameter. As a result, MWCNTs exhibit diameters that are several tens of nm and lengths equal to several tens of microns or more, in contrast to SWCNTs, which have a diameter of about 1 nm and lengths extending to a couple microns or even more[7]. CNTs are great nanostructures that appeal to biological agents because of their high surface area, stiff structure, and superior electrical conductivity. Because of this, biological uses for carbon nanotubes have recently become more prevalent, including biosensing, bioimaging, and drug transport [6,10]. One of the crystallised forms of carbon is called graphene. One can create a number of physiologically and medically significant derivatives from graphene, including graphite oxide, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and nano-graphene oxide (nano-GO). A layered arrangement of "graphene oxide" sheets produces the inorganic, strongly hydrophilic substance known as graphite oxide. Contrarily, graphite oxide is often treated with an acid or a base, then subjected to sonication, to produce GO. When treated with a reducing chemical, GO's reduced electrical conductivity can be enhanced. However, GO solubility in organic conditions is increased by chemical modification [9,11]. Outstanding mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties characterise graphene. Additionally, novel biotechnological approaches to examine living cells are being developed using their cytotoxicity to trigger selective cell death. Utilised in drug and gene delivery, and biosensing [11]. A lively category of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials called CDs. They have polymer groups on the surface of a tiny, crystal-like graphitized core. Due to their small size (1–10 nm), distinctive optical features, simplicity of functionalization, and biological compatibility, CDs have become increasingly used in biological and medical fields over the past ten years. They can pass through a number of in vivo biological barriers because to their small size, including ion channels, the blood-brain barrier (BBB), and the barrier of glomerular filtration [12]. The use of CDs in biology and biomedicine makes use of properties including excellent QYs of fluorescence, chiral luminescence, up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), and NIR emission. CDs and CDs-based nanomaterials offer the greatest potential for use in a variety of biological and biomedical fields, particularly imaging and therapies, because of their distinct chemical, physical, and optical capabilities. Carbon dots, for example, are used in labeling and tracking specific molecules within living cells because of their fluorescent properties, allowing researchers to observe cellular processes with exclusive clarity. Similarly, carbon nanotubes and graphene-based materials are used as contrast agents in various imaging modalities, including MRI and CT scans, to provide detailed anatomical information and aid in disease detection [13]. Fullerenes with several layers are also referred to as carbon nano-onions (CNO) or onion-like nanocarbons (ONC). CNO could have a range of characteristics that make them particularly interesting for biological applications [14]. In addition to having exceptional physical and chemical characteristics, CNHs and CNOs can be used for fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging. CNHs, which resemble the conical and single walled carbon nanostructures related to CNTs, and CNOs, which are composed of multiple spherical graphitic layers of carbon, typically contain C60 fullerene or larger equivalents in their centres [6]. One promising avenue in biomedicine is the development of novel synthetic approaches for two-dimensional metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as proposed by Pal et al. [15]. These MOFs hold great promise for applications in drug delivery, biosensing, and tissue engineering due to their tunable properties and high surface area. By leveraging innovative synthetic strategies, researchers aim to design MOF-based nanomaterials with tailored functionalities for targeted biomedical applications. Furthermore, nanofibers fabricated using spinning and nano-spinning techniques, as discussed by Pal et al. [16], have emerged as promising materials for biomedical applications. These nanofibers exhibit high surface area, porosity, and mechanical strength, making them suitable for applications in wound healing, drug delivery, and tissue regeneration. By exploring advanced fabrication techniques, researchers seek to develop nanofiber-based scaffolds and implants for regenerative medicine and tissue engineering applications. In the field of biosensing and bioimaging, carbon-based nanomaterials offer unparalleled opportunities for high-resolution imaging and sensitive detection of biomolecules. Pal et al. [17] critically reviewed the emerging avenue of nanographene, highlighting its potential applications in nano-imaging and biosensing. Nanographene-based materials exhibit unique optical and electronic properties, enabling non-invasive imaging of biological structures and sensitive detection of biomarkers for early disease diagnosis and monitoring. In this review, we have focused on carbon-based nanomaterials usage in bio imaging and bio sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon based nanomaterials in bio imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the composition and physiological processes of cells and organisms is made possible through bioimaging. As a result, it is a crucial tool for making diagnoses of human diseases. Optical bioimaging is one of the many bioimaging techniques that is getting more and more attention. The method makes use of optical contrast, or the variation in optical characteristics between the imaged area and the background. This method enables early identification, screening, diagnosis, and therapy of fatal diseases like cancer while also allowing imaging at the cellular and even single-molecule level [13]. For use in biology and medicine, a fluorescent material must exhibit exceptional biocompatibility, strong quantum yield, biosafety, and photostability in order to become a bioimaging agent. These biological imaging agents rely on the localisation of the particular nanoparticles in cells which protects the patient from being exposed to damaging radiation for the disease diagnosis and/or for treatment and acts as an alternative method of diagnosis or treatment [18]. It is impossible to deny the significant significance that bioimaging technology plays in clinical diagnosis, and treatment. Recently, techniques including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), ultrasound imaging (US), have been investigated and used to monitor and forecast disease [19].

Carbon nanotubes, or CNTs, have remarkable mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties. They are cylindrical structures made of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. For the production of CNTs, a variety of synthesis methods are used, utilizing catalysts like transition metals and carbon sources like hydrocarbons. Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), Arc Discharge, Chemical Vapor Condensation (CVC), Laser Ablation, and Template-Assisted Growth are some of these techniques. Every technique produces CNTs with unique diameter, length, and structure, making them useful in a variety of industries such as electronics, materials science, and biomedicine [20]. Morphological analysis of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in bioimaging entails a thorough investigation of their structural characteristics and how they affect the effectiveness of imaging in biological settings. Research by Refs. [21,22] highlight how important it is to consider factors like CNT chirality, diameter, and length because these affect not only their electronic and optical behaviors but also their in vivo distribution and clearance rates. Surface functionalization, as discussed in [23,24], emerges as a critical aspect in augmenting CNTs' biocompatibility and stability through chemical modifications or PEGylation, enhancing their circulation and cellular uptake while mitigating nonspecific interactions. Furthermore, optical properties, as detailed in [21], including absorption, emission spectra, and photostability, are instrumental in enabling multiplexed imaging and ensuring prolonged signal detection reliability. Insights from studies like [25,24] elucidate biological interactions such as cellular uptake mechanisms and in vivo behavior, shedding light on CNTs' compatibility and utility as imaging agents. Evaluation of imaging performance metrics such as contrast enhancement and spatial resolution, as addressed in [21,22], provides crucial guidance for optimizing CNT-based imaging agents for enhanced visualization and diagnostic accuracy. Additionally, Refs. [25,23] underscore the necessity of rigorous toxicity and biocompatibility assessments to ensure the safe deployment of CNTs in biomedical applications, thereby informing their long-term viability for imaging purposes. By integrating findings from these studies into morphological analyses, researchers can advance the development of CNT-based bioimaging technologies with improved efficacy, biocompatibility, and safety profiles.

The visualisation of cellular uptake and distribution of CNTs has been investigated using a variety of techniques, primarily Near-infrared imaging linked to the distinctive intrinsic fluorescence of CNTs, Raman imaging originating from the distinctive Raman scattering of CNTs, and Visible wavelength fluorescence imaging made possible by the covalent linking of organic fluorophores [26].

There is very little signal interference as a result of the modifications in CNTs occurring in the near-infrared (NIR) region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Due to its decreased photo-absorption and minimal auto-fluorescent background, the infrared spectrum between 950 and 1200 nm is a crucial optical frame for biomedical applications. Since there is less absorption in the chosen band of the spectrum, optical signals can travel farther. Additionally, CNTs have outstanding photostability [21,24,27]. Welsher combined the biocompatibility of single walled nano tubes (SWNTs) disseminated in phospholipid-polyethylene glycol (PL-PEG) with the superior relative quantum yield of SWNTs distributed in sodium cholate. In comparison to SWNTs directly suspended in PL-PEG, the relative quantum yield of these exchange-SWNTs has increased by over one order of magnitude. These exchange-SWNT conjugates were then employed as in vivo NIR photoluminescence imaging agents in live mice and as an NIR photoluminescence contrast agent for targeted cell imaging. For whole-mouse imaging, exchange SWNTs showed that they could obtain good image contrast at a relatively modest dose (15 mg l-1). Additionally, tumour-bearing mice underwent high-magnification NIR photoluminescence imaging, demonstrating the capability to discern tiny capillaries within the tumour [28]. This work validates the use of brilliant biocompatible SWNTs as adaptable in vivo NIR photoluminescence imaging agents for living animals given their previously established minimal toxicity and RES excretion [29]. SWNTs have been demonstrated by Welsher to be helpful fluorophores in the low-albedo NIR II region. These fluorophores are sufficiently brilliant to allow rapid frame rate imaging deep inside mice without the use of unnecessary excitation power. Due to the low autofluorescence and tissue scattering, fluorescent imaging in the second near-infrared window (NIR II, 1000–1400 nm) has a lot of potential. Here, high-frame-rate video imaging of mice was conducted while being intravenously injected with well-functionalized, biocompatible single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs), in order to study the course of SWNTs in the mouse anatomy [30,31].

One of the most effective and promising CNT-based bioimaging techniques is Raman imaging [6]. Raman bioimaging is a photon scattering mechanism as opposed to photoluminescence and involves the emission of photons with modified wavelengths upon light excitation. SWCNTs provide higher resonance. A very helpful technique for bioimaging and sensing is Raman scattering [32]. The location and mapping of nanotubes inside cells can be done without the use of labels by using spectral properties related to the many vibration modes of the C-C bonds in a CNT [33,34]. Additionally effective in vivo and non-intrusive MRI has been employed extensively in medical applications. MRI can be utilised in a non-invasive way to distinguish between abnormal and healthy tissues [35]. The contrast agent used in MRI, such as gadolinium (III) salts, nitroxide radicals or iron oxide, must include magnetic atoms [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives in bio imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional carbon compound graphene has a honeycomb structure of lattices with tightly packed single-layer carbon atoms [37]. Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), mechanical exfoliation, and liquid-phase exfoliation are some of the techniques used in the synthesis of graphene and its derivatives. Typically, procedures like CVD or exfoliation techniques are used to create graphene from graphite. Graphite oxide is converted into graphene derivatives, such as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), by oxidation and reduction processes, respectively. These techniques use metal substrates or chemical reagents as catalysts in addition to carbon sources like graphite. These methods of synthesis produce graphene and its derivatives with specific properties that make them useful for a wide range of biomedical, electronics, and energy applications [38].

Morphological analysis of graphene and its derivatives in bioimaging involves a thorough examination of their structural characteristics and their impact on imaging applications in biological contexts. Insights from Refs. such as [39,40] contribute to this analysis. A single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, graphene is incredibly strong and electrically conductible. Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO), two of its derivatives, are modified with functional groups or reduced oxygen content to change their properties for particular imaging applications. Their compatibility and targeting specificity within biological environments are improved by surface modifications using biomolecules or targeting ligands. Graphene-based materials are useful as contrast agents or imaging probes because of their optical qualities, which include strong light absorption and tunable fluorescence. This ensures consistent imaging over a long period of time. Optimizing imaging contrast and reducing cytotoxicity are made easier by having a thorough understanding of cellular uptake mechanisms and in vivo behavior. The assessment of imaging metrics, such as spatial resolution and contrast enhancement, directs their application in high-resolution bioimaging. Toxicity evaluations also guide the creation of safe imaging agents, guaranteeing their long-term applicability and biocompatibility in biomedical contexts. Researchers can improve the design and application of graphene and its derivatives for bioimaging, improving imaging performance and biocompatibility for a variety of biomedical applications, by incorporating findings from these references into morphological analysis.

Optical imaging is a non-invasive technology that takes precise images of organs, tissues, and even tiny objects like cells and molecules using visible light and the unique characteristics of photons [41]. Its advantages over other methods of imaging include being very inexpensive, highly sensitive, non-ionizing radiation, capable of real-time imaging, and having the capacity to multiplex [42]. Nanomaterials based on graphene have been extensively explored for such optical imaging methods such as fluorescence and two-photon FL imaging. A lot of research has been done on graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) modified with dyes, quantum dots (QDs), and gold nanoparticle clusters (GNCs) for FL imaging [41]. According to Dai et al., nGO's inherent photoluminescence can be exploited for fluorescence imaging. Rituxan (anti-CD20), a B cell-specific antibody, was chemically coupled with PEG-GO by these authors to create nGO-PEG-Rituxan for B cell-targeted fluorescent imaging with little background [43]. Due to their inherent photoluminescence, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be used as photoluminescence probes for FL imaging. The effectiveness of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) as a two-photon fluorescence probe for TPFI is demonstrated by their strong two-photon absorption cross section, which reaches up to 50,000 Goppert-Mayer units [41]. D, G, and 2D bands from intrinsic resonance Raman scattering in GO are sufficiently enough to distinguish it from fluorescence and other materials backgrounds. According to reports, after unique metal nanoparticles were deposited on GO's basal plane, the D and G bands were amplified [44]. GO is therefore appropriate for use as a Raman reporter in the highly sensitive identification and imaging of biologic molecules because it exhibits clear and powerful intrinsic Raman signals [42,45].

For in vivo and non-intrusive cellular imaging, GO-magnetic nanoparticle composite is employed to improve the MRI signal [39]. Manganese (Mn) and gadolinium (Gd) are two paramagnetic metal ions that exhibit considerable toxicity due to their nonselective interaction with biomolecules. By enclosing the hazardous ions between layers of graphene, which lessen their toxic effects, GO with various functional groups that contain oxygen can be readily chelated with these ions [46,47].

Carbon-based quantum dots include graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new class of luminous materials, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as carbon dots, C-dots, or CDs [48]. Xu et al. inadvertently discovered CDs while using arc-discharge techniques to separate and purify single-walled carbon nanotubes [49]. Carbonaceous precursors such as organic molecules or carbon-containing polymers are used to produce carbon dots (CDs). Hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, pyrolysis, electrochemical synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and ultrasonic-assisted synthesis are examples of common techniques. These techniques create CDs through heating or chemical reactions, frequently with the help of surface passivation agents for increased stability. CDs are useful for applications in bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and photocatalysis because they can be customized with specific properties like size, surface chemistry, and fluorescence emission [50].

Due to their excellent water dispersibility, adjustable emission, low toxicity, excellent photostability, and outstanding biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs), a new and well-developed class of carbon nanomaterials, have garnered a lot of attention [51]. CDs' strong photoluminescence capabilities and low toxicity make them a desirable choice for bioimaging in addition to being useful for biosensing. In in vivo and in vitro optical imaging research, conventional QDs, such as CdTe and similar core-shell nanoparticles, have been extensively exploited. Moreover, CDs are ideal for cellular imaging due to their strong photostability and biocompatibility [6,52,53].

Due to the low penetration of light at such short wavelengths in biological tissues, imaging with conventional Ultraviolet-absorbing and blue/green-emitting CDs suffers from substantial background disturbance. To achieve adequate light penetration depth and reduce photodamage to cellular tissues, it is crucial to produce CDs with near-infrared emission qualities for bioimaging [13].

The potential use of NIR-CDs as near-infrared emission probes for bioimaging is anticipated. The cytotoxicity of NIR-CDs with Hela cells was assessed using the conventional MTT test. Even after being incubated with NIR-CDs for twenty-four hours at a rather substantial concentration of 300 g mL-1, Hela cells exhibit no toxicity towards them. To assess the NIR-CDs' potential for bio-imaging, Hela cells were incubated with them and viewed under bright-field conditions using a confocal fluorescence microscope. A 540 nm excitation wavelength was chosen. NIR-CDs have outstanding cell labelling capabilities as demonstrated by their fast penetration of Hela cells and production of strong near-infrared emission at the cytoplasm. Due to its negligible impact on biological tissues, deep tissue penetration, extremely low autofluorescence, and effective spatial imaging capabilities, near-IR fluorescence imaging has received a lot of interest. New near-infrared probes called NIR-CDs have enormous promise for application in biological fields [54,55].

Additionally, CDs are applicable to photoacoustic (PA) imaging. The innovative bioimaging technique known as PA combines light excitation and ultrasonic detection to enable imaging beyond the optical diffusion limit. It offers good spatial resolution and rather deep tissue penetration [13].

CNOs are multi shell fullerenes. surface Due to their strong cellular uptake and simple cell-penetration capabilities, they are easily absorbed by cells and are small enough to be transported in the circulatory system [56,57].

Due of their hydrophobic carbon-based nature brought on by molecular grouping, CNO display limited solubility in water as well as in a number of organic solvents. Therefore, it is unknown how important they are biologically. However, due to their distinct form and tiny size, CNO may have a number of characteristics that make them particularly valuable for biological applications involving cell internalisation, presumably by tissue diffusion. Because of its quasi spherical shape, CNO interacts well with cell membranes, and its surface enables biological molecules to be further enhanced to enable cell imaging [14].

Frasconi modified CNOs by chemically oxidising the flaws in the carbon nanoparticles' outer shell, which resulted in significant carboxyl group surface functionalization. The process that produced fluorescently labelled CNOs involved the creation of an amide bond between the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the CNOs and fluoresceinamine. Because of the existence of an extensive surface coverage of groups of carboxylic acid, the functionalized CNOs exhibit strong emission properties and water dispersibility, which translate into an effective fluorescent probe used for in vitro imaging of HeLa cells without considerable cytotoxicity. Due to its high water dispersibility, effective internalisation by cancer cells, and localization in particular cell compartments, the resulting nanomaterial represents a viable platform for biological imaging applications [14,58,59]. These functionalized CNOs are attractive candidates for fluorescent probes in bioimaging research because to have excellent biocompatibility, low systemic toxicity, and effective cellular absorption [60–62].

The development of a CNO-based bioimaging probe focuses on three distinct areas. First, it is necessary to design the synthetic methods for the production of a superior fluorescent/non-fluorescent CNO with a preferred narrow size distribution. Second, subsequent functionalization allows them to accommodate targeted molecules after their initial stepwise functionalization makes them water-soluble. Third, in order to use biological systems as a bioimaging probe for extended periods of time, it is necessary to investigate the cytotoxicity effect of CNO on these systems [14].

Due to their great drug-loading capacity and huge surface area compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials, CNHs are the ideal options for bio-applications. In addition, a combination of pentagons, hexagons, and heptagons are used to create the CNH structure. Considered to be more chemically reactive locations, the carbon atoms in pentagonal cells can be further enhanced by targeting compound. For the synthesis of CNHs, a number of techniques have been reported, including the CO2 laser ablation of graphite rods, the arc discharge approach, and the Joule heating method. CNHs can be chemically oxidised using HNO3 acid or through prolonged heat treatment with O2 or with CO2 [63,64].

SWCNTs and single-walled carbon nano horns (CNHs) are linked [63]. These are conical structures with lengths of 45–60 nm and widths of 2.5–6 nm, which frequently coalesce into aggregates resembling dahlia flowers or buds and having a diameter of about 95 nm. CNHs have a large surface area as well as high porosity, which can be used for biomedical purposes. [60,64,65].

CNHs can be chemically oxidised using HNO3 acid or through prolonged heat treatment with O2 or CO2. With greater biocompatibility and a "dahlia-like" shape, CNHs are created through the chemical oxidation process. Additionally, this approach makes it easier for CNH to become covalently functionalized. Chemically oxidised CNHs have so drawn a lot of interest as drug delivery systems and bioimaging tools. A straightforward hydrothermal chemical oxidation procedure was used to create "dahlia-like" hydrophilic fluorescent carbon nano horns (HFCNHs) at a low temperature of 100°C. Because of structural flaws like 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMFs) or similar aromatic compounds produced during Nafion-encapsulated carbon nano rice(NCNR) oxidation, the HFCNHs obtained could be employed as bio-imaging probes [64].

For the very first time, a nanocomposite made of single walled CNHs and NaYF4 : Yb,Er amino-modified luminous nanoparticles was created by Xu et al. This nanocomposite was effectively employed as an agent for the imaging of HeLa cells after being covalently linked with anti-CEA8 antibody of rabbit. [66].

A gadget with a biological sensing component is known as a biosensor. An electrical transducer is coupled to the biological sensing component. The intensity of the signal is proportional to the concentration of a certain chemical or combination of chemicals, and a transducer transforms an observable physical or chemical change into a measurable signal (for example, an electrical signal). The materials used in the construction of biosensors affect how well they operate [67,68]. Applications for biosensors include everything from prosthetic devices to checking the quality of food [67]. Due to their ease of use during construction, low background current, capacity to regenerate surfaces, and low cost, carbon-based nano materials including carbon nano tubes (CNTs), CNO, CHN, and graphene oxide (GO) are frequently utilised to create biosensors. These materials have simple biomolecular coupling capabilities on the surface, reproducible electrochemical behaviour, and practical physical properties. Some carbon materials' high conductivity and porosity make them perfect for adsorbing big molecules (such as enzyme complexes) and electrochemical signal transmission without the need to chemically alter the enzyme. Due to their distinctive biological and physical characteristics, carbon nanomaterials have attracted the attention of researchers in recent years [68].

Because of their benefits, such as a high signal-to-noise ratio, low background, high sensitivity, label-free detection, continuous monitoring in real time, and simplicity of apparatus, biosensing based on CNTs has garnered considerable interest from scientists. Additionally, CNTs have an extremely large surface area that may be loaded with several molecules to provide multiplexed sensing. In addition, CNTs linked to proteins or nucleic acids [6]. The immobilisation of biological molecules on the surface of CNTs is the fundamental component of CNT-based biosensors, which improves signal transduction and identification. These biosensors can be broadly divided into optical and electrochemical CNT-based biosensors depending on their target identification and transduction processes. Combining electrochemical and electronic biosensors is a good use for CNTs, which are regarded as promising materials for enhancing electron transmission [69–72].

Carbon nanotube non-woven fabrics (CNTFs) have been used to create a variety of CNT-glucose biosensors based on the conjugation of glucose oxidase to sense glucose from a glucose oxidase-impregnated polyvinyl alcohol solution. Nitric oxide and epinephrine sensing electrochemical biosensors based on CNTs have also been developed. For the purpose of identifying human blood proteins, 20 different SWCNT corona phases were generated. The study showed that the particular corona phase had a strong selectivity for fibrinogen recognition and caused a more than eighty five percent reduction in SWCNT florescence intensity at saturation. However, there was barely any red shift, and absorption intensity remained constant. The excitation-emission characteristics of the SWCNT sample before and after the fibrinogen addition showed that the fluorescence response of SWCNT with a smaller diameter was more apparent in comparison to the nanotube with a greater diameter. The environment of human blood serum was used to test the fibrinogen recognition [70].

Apparently biocompatible CNTs are necessary for effective Surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensing of biological things [73]. Since CNTs are free of any metallic contaminants, effective immobilisation of biological molecules has been shown to be unstable. It is challenging to precisely construct and incorporate electronic components onto CNTs because of their one-dimensional nature. Through traditional microfabrication techniques, one-dimensional CNT integration with valuable metal nanomaterials in intricate SERS sensors or alternative devices has advanced significantly. The CNT-silver (Ag) NPs were used by the Tsvetkov group as SERS substrates made using an electrochemical deposition technique, and they attained an enhancement factor (EF) of 50-2000.

The creation of several hot spots increases the Raman signal by up to 50000, and the shape of CNTs coated with solid Ag film encourages substantial inconsistent localisation of the light radiation impinge on the substrate. A wide range of potential for extremely sensitive SERS molecular testing is demonstrated by CNT-Ag's exceptional performance [73,74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and its derivatives in biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and its derivatives can act as energy donors or acceptors in a Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensor depending on their quenching and fluorescence characteristics. They have been thoroughly studied for the identification of single-base mismatches, the measurement of the melting of DNA duplexes, the sensing of biomolecules [6].

GR composite materials are used to build biosensors and have a variety of functions. Due to the outstanding qualities of graphene and its derivatives, such as its structure, huge surface area, biocompatibility, and good adsorption capabilities, they have a lot of potential uses in biosensing [75,76]. To create GR-inorganic nanocomposites, inorganic nanoparticles can be disseminated on the surface of GR sheets [77,78]. To maintain the structure and characteristics of the monolayer GR, inorganic NPs can widen the space between the layers and lessen the force between them. This complementary effect is crucial for the applications [79,80].

Using batch adsorption tests, Liu and colleagues investigated the effects of two inorganic NPs, silicon dioxide and aluminium oxide, on the process of adsorption of 17-estradiol onto GR oxide [81]. The outcomes showed that inorganic NPs considerably slow down adsorption and lengthen the time needed for reaching adsorption equilibrium. Thus, this work offers fresh information about the dispersal and movement of GR and contaminants in naturally occurring aquatic habitats [37]. Single- and double-stranded DNA can be detected electrochemically and selectively using graphene nanoparticles [82]. The strong ionic contact between the positively charged nucleobases and the negatively charged -COOH groups , the good electrochemical characteristics of graphene, and the robust - layering between the nucleobases and honeycomb carbon structure could all be responsible for the high sensitivity [70]. Many plasmonic sensors with plasmonic metal layers for sensitivity enhancement have been suggested by employing graphene and its derivatives. The base of the prism was covered with layers of biomolecules, graphene, silicon, and gold to create the sensor model. Both the silicon and graphene layers were employed to increase sensitivity, while the gold served as a plasmonic layer. The graphene layer aids in improving biomolecule immobilisation in addition to improving sensitivity [83].

In Fig. 4, graphene nanomaterials are integrated with a prism-based Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) probe to improve sensing performance. The metal film of the SPR probe is coated with graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms. Because of graphene's high conductivity and large surface area, this addition increases detection and stability. Like with conventional SPR probes, the working principle involves tracking variations in SPR angle or wavelength to identify changes in refractive index. Label-free biosensing and environmental monitoring are two applications that take advantage of graphene's sensitivity and benefits for real-time monitoring [84].

Zhang and colleagues created polyaniline (PANI) and carboxyl (-COOH) functionalized GO. They were able to detect DNA [85]. Tang described a GO-based fluorescence quenching recovery sensor for detecting ssDNA with a limit of detection (LOD) of nano meter range for DNA detection. Their sensors work even when DNAase is present because ssDNA maintained on the surface of GO was indigestible [86].

A brand-new cluster of carbon atoms with a particle size smaller than 10 nm is known as a carbon dot (CD). Interesting fluorescence (FL) characteristics are also present in CDs. Due of its intriguing properties, including as minimal toxicity, excellent water solubility, and exceptional biocompatibility, CDs are appealing [87].

Numerous benefits of photoluminescence, non-toxicity, chemical inertness, and a wealth of functional groups are included in CD biosensors [88]. To detect bioactive chemicals such amino acids, proteins, nucleic acids, enzymes, vitamins, bacteria, biological pigments plant part-derived CDs have been used as biosensors [87].

For the purpose of specific sensing of L-Cys over all other amino acids, Vandarkuzhali et al. used pineapple peel CDs [89]. CDs-based biosensors can be divided into three groups: fluorescence shift, on-off, and off-on. The most popular sensing design for sensors using CDs is the on-off category (also known as fluorescence quenching). The method has been used to find different cations, anions, and tiny molecules. The "off-on" technique makes use of a rise in CDs' fluorescence intensity when an analyte is present. The radiometric detection of analytes is made possible by the partial spectrum interference between donor emission and acceptor absorption. By contrasting the intensities at two emission wavelengths, the concentration of the analyte can be expressed as a ratio [13,90–92].

Due to their excellent conductivity and biological compatibility, carbon nano-onions are developing electrode materials in biosensing. In addition to having a high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent conductivity, thermal stability, and biological compatibility, CNOs are hollow spherical nanoparticles made of concentric layers of graphene that have increasing diameters. For a variety of applications, such as storing energy, biological imaging, sensing, etc., they are able to be modified by an extensive variety of functional groups and are incorporated into composite substances with organic or inorganic polymers [93,94]. By covalently immobilising an enzyme conjugate on a chitosan matrix, it was possible to create an extremely sensitive biological sensor for glyphosate that relies on the suppression of tyrosinase activity. Due to an increased active surface area and improved electron transfer capabilities offered by the conductive nano particles, the existence of CNOs on the chitosan-enzyme composite beneficially influenced the sensitivity of the biological sensors [94].

Recently, it was discovered that carbon nano-onions (CNOs) are good substances for electrodes for the detection of glyphosate, antibodies, and small compounds. Concentric graphitic layers of increasing diameter are what make up CNOs, which also have other intriguing qualities including excellent conductivity and biocompatibility. For a variety of applications, including storing energy and biomedicine, they can be readily changed by a wide range of functional groups and included in composite substances with either inorganic or organic polymers [93].

CNOs were initially utilised by Bartelmess and his colleagues to create electrochemical-based biosensors [95]. CNOs were employed by Bartolome et al. to identify the human papilloma oncogene using the Glassy carbon electrodes(GCE) surface [96,97]. In order to detect the cancer biomarker CA19-9, Gisela Ibáez-Redn created a capacitive immunosensor employing inexpensive screen-printed interdigitated electrodes (SPIDEs) modified with GO and CNO films. Using straightforward and affordable materials, the manufacturing technique enabled the creation of several hundred SPIDEs in a short amount of time. The device's analytical properties were improved by modification with nanostructured CNOs and GO films, resulting in a simple and repeatable platform that is capable of detecting CA19-9 with a permissible limit of detection of 0.15 U mL-1 [98,99]. In order to build an amperometric enzyme biosensor for phenoxy herbicides based on Horseradish peroxidase(HRP) immobilised on CNOs and then cross-linked with a cyclodextrin polymer, Sok and Fragaso investigated the use of CNO-modified electrodes [100–102]. The turn-off and turn-on method of the photoluminescence sensor, which was developed for the purpose of selectively determining glucose utilising water-soluble CNOs [103–105]. Quenching of fluorescence serves as the foundation for CD-based analyte sensing. Different chemical interactions between the quenching analyte and the fluorophoric CDs, such as excited-state reaction, and ground-state complex formation, energy transfer or electron transfer, and collision can result in the quenching or reduction of fluorescence in QY. The quenching mechanisms can be broadly divided into two types: dynamic type, which is brought on by collision, and static type, which is brought on by the creation of a ground-state complex between the CDs and the analyte species [106–108]. CNOs also helped to increase their repeatability and stability. In order to create mobile pesticide detection devices for in-field agricultural and environmental uses, this approach can be further developed [93].

Carbon Nano horns (CNHs), one type of carbon allotrope, are 45–60 nm long closed cages of carbon atoms with a diameter of 2.5–6 nm [109]. Nano horns can be created in large quantities and do not require a metal catalyst during their creation. In a variety of applications, they offer a practical and beneficial substitute for carbon nanotubes as well as graphene. The major uses of carbon nano horns involve their abilities to adsorb gases or other molecules in order to serve as carriers for drugs, to build efficient biological sensors and electrodes for electrochemical as well as photoelectrochemical reasons, or as a support catalysts and function as fuel cells. The huge surface area and superior porosity of the CNHs can be utilised for their prospective use in the biosensing industry [110,111]. SWCNHs, or single walled carbon nano horns, are frequently utilised as electrochemical sensing electrode materials. Essentially, due to their greater conductivity, SWCNHs can be used directly for the manufacture of electrodes (Figs. 1-5
, and Table 1). Furthermore, rather of being dispersed separately, they have the ability to self-aggregate to create 70–100 nm spherical aggregates with various morphologies, such as dahlia, seed, and bud morphologies. The dahlia-shaped SWCNHs have the most noticeable horn-like structure among them, whereas the bud-like structure has a tiny tube structure devoid of horn-like structure. Additionally, the outermost layer of the SWCNHs that resemble seeds is richly folded. Due to their distinctive characteristics, such as their enormous surface area, multiple interior nano spaces, high electrical conductivity, and mechanical robustness, these unusual forms create the perfect materials for resistive, fluorescent, and electrochemical sensors as well as other types of sensing devices [112]. Since no metal catalyst is used in their manufacture by laser ablation on extremely purified graphite, single-walled carbon nano horns (SWCNH) are a type of nanocarbon with a high degree of purity. According to Han et al., an a molecular imprinted electrochemical sensor for detecting the presence of kanamycin was created by covering a polished glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a slurry of SWCNHs-COOH [113].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion, key challenges and future aspects
ce:section.ce:para: To summarize, advanced carbon nanomaterials provide a multifunctional approach to improving bioimaging and biosensors. Their exceptional properties, such as high electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and optical transparency, have contributed to significant progress in these fields. Carbon nanomaterial synthesis and customization have resulted in tailored designs for specific bioimaging and biosensing applications. Nonetheless, several challenges remain in the widespread use and optimization of advanced carbon nanomaterials for bioimaging and biosensor applications. A significant challenge is ensuring the scalability and reproducibility of synthesis methods, as many advanced carbon nanomaterials necessitate complex and expensive processes. Furthermore, ensuring the biocompatibility and long-term stability of carbon nanomaterials in biological environments is critical for practical applications. Furthermore, incorporating carbon nanomaterials into current imaging and sensing platforms while maintaining sensitivity and specificity is a significant technical challenge. Despite these challenges, the future of advanced carbon nanomaterials in bioimaging and biosensors looks very promising. Ongoing research efforts to develop scalable synthesis methods, refine surface functionalization techniques, and assess biocompatibility will address current limitations and enable wider adoption. Furthermore, advances in nanotechnology, material science, and biotechnology will propel the development of new carbon nanomaterial-based imaging probes and biosensors with enhanced performance and functionality. The groundbreaking aspect of these applications lies in their capacity to transform biomedical research and clinical practices. Using the unique properties of carbon nanomaterials, researchers can develop novel approaches for early disease detection, precise therapy delivery, and continuous monitoring of biological processes. Collaborative interdisciplinary efforts among scientists, engineers, and medical professionals will be critical for translating laboratory discoveries into practical applications for clinical diagnostics, therapeutics, and biomedical research.

Sai Nandhini Ravi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Sasireka Rajendran: Validation. G.S. Madhumathi: Writing – original draft. Azhagu Saravana Babu Packirisamy: Methodology, Formal analysis. Sugumari Vallinayagam: Visualization, Validation. Azmat Ali Khan: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Conceptualization. Abdul Malik: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis.
